parentQuestion: Sour taste receptors are stimulated by hydrogen ions of acidic food substances.

scaffolds.q: Acids release hydrogen ions (H+) when dissolved in solution.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Acids by definition donate H+ ions. More acidic = more H+ ions = lower pH.

scaffolds.q: pH measures hydrogen ion concentration — lower pH means MORE H+ ions.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! pH 2 (very acidic) has many more H+ ions than pH 7 (neutral).

scaffolds.q: Taste receptor cells can respond to specific ions or molecules.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Different taste receptors detect different chemical signals.

scaffolds.q: When ions enter taste receptor cells, they can cause depolarization.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Ion entry changes the cell's electrical state, triggering taste signals.

scaffolds.q: Lemon juice and vinegar taste sour because they are acidic.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Their acidity releases H+ ions that stimulate sour receptors.

scaffolds.q: Sour taste intensity increases as pH decreases (more acidic).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! More H+ ions = stronger sour signal. The relationship is roughly logarithmic.

scaffolds.q: H+ ions can enter sour receptor cells through ion channels, causing depolarization.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! H+ enters through PKD2L1 channels and other pathways, directly exciting the cell.

scaffolds.q: Sour taste may have evolved to detect spoiled food (bacterial acid production).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Bacteria produce acids during fermentation — sour detection warns of spoilage.

scaffolds.q: Sour taste receptors are directly stimulated by hydrogen ions from acidic foods.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! H+ ions are the direct chemical stimulus for sour taste. Acid → H+ → receptor depolarization → sour perception.
